ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai garlic black pepper beef stir-fry with jasmine rice. FOODON_00002725 (beef dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Thai-style garlic black pepper beef stir-fry with rice as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Thai composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Thai garlic black pepper beef with jasmine rice, approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 150 g of stir-fried beef in sauce and 300 g of cooked jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried with garlic and black pepper sauce, with steamed jasmine rice — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean only, cooked, broiled (NDB 13163); Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked (NDB 20045); and seasoning/sauce component estimated from USDA FoodData Central branded and SR Legacy condiment entries.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai garlic black pepper beef with jasmine rice exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite: approximately 33% cooked lean beef (USDA SR Legacy NDB 13163), 67% cooked jasmine rice (USDA SR Legacy NDB 20045), with an additive sodium and fat increment to account for garlic-black-pepper sauce (oyster sauce, soy sauce, fish sauce, oil). The composite per-100g values reflect the plated dish as served. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (jasmine-type, cooked) GI approximately 64 (glucose reference); composite dish GI dominated by jasmine rice component.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai garlic black pepper beef with jasmine rice as a composite dish. The GI of 64 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source — cooked jasmine rice — which has a well-established GI of approximately 64 (medium) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The beef and sauce components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 96.75) / 100 ≈ 62.0 g, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 21.5 × 4.5 = 96.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 380 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,710 mg per standard 450 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as a standard 450 g serving delivers an estimated glycemic load of approximately 62 g driven by the jasmine rice component (GI 64, medium); portion reduction, substitution with brown rice or cauliflower rice, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies to moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and elevated sodium from sauce components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation; the saturated fat content from beef (approximately 1.0 g per 100 g) and the high sodium load from the garlic-black-pepper sauce (approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g) both warrant attention, and choosing leaner beef cuts and requesting reduced-sodium sauce preparation can help mitigate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Garlic Black Pepper Beef with Jasmine Rice is a Thai stir-fried beef dish in which tender slices of beef are wok-tossed over high heat with generous amounts of fresh garlic and coarsely ground black pepper in a savoury sauce typically composed of oyster sauce, light soy sauce, fish sauce, and a small amount of sugar. The dish is served over a bed of steamed fragrant jasmine rice. The flavour profile is bold, savoury, and peppery with a subtle sweetness from the oyster sauce and the natural umami of the beef. The texture of the beef is tender and slightly charred at the edges from wok hei (the characteristic smoky breath of the wok). As a composite dish, the nutritional profile per 100 g is estimated at approximately 148 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 3.2 g fat, and 21.5 g carbohydrate, with sodium approximately 380 mg per 100 g — elevated due to the sauce components. The glycemic index of the dish is driven by the jasmine rice component (GI approximately 64, medium category), resulting in a high glycemic load per standard serving. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension due to its high sodium content, and for those with Type 2 Diabetes due to the high glycemic load from jasmine rice. It is moderately concerning for cardiovascular disease due to combined saturated fat and sodium. The dish is a popular Thai restaurant offering in Singapore, commonly found at Thai casual dining establishments such as Saap Saap Thai.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this composite Thai dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked lean beef and cooked jasmine rice with an additive sodium and fat increment for the sauce component. GI is assigned from the jasmine rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for estimation for this composite dish. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values where defensible from the composite source approach. The dish is sold at Saap Saap Thai (Funan, Singapore) under the menu name 'RD2 Thai Garlic Black Pepper Double Beef'; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Thai Garlic Black Pepper Beef with Jasmine Rice' for database purposes. Halal status is listed as false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; consumers should verify directly with the restaurant. The glycemic load per serving is calculated based on a 450 g serving size: (64 × 96.75) / 100 ≈ 62.0.
